rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical difluoromethylation of electron-rich olefins (enamides and styrenes) using HCF2SO2Na as the CF2H radical source in an undivided cell, giving (E)-difluoromethylated enamides or β-hydroxy-difluoromethylated styrene products.
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of CF2HSO2Na (sodium difluoromethanesulfinate) generates the CF2H radical via loss of SO2; a second anodic oxidation of the alkyl radical adduct gives a benzylic/α-aminocarbocation; cathode reduces H+ to H2.

key_intermediates.name: CF2H radical (from anodic oxidation of HCF2SO2- and loss of SO2)
α-amino/benzylic C-radical I from CF2H addition to electron-rich alkene
benzylic/α-amino carbocation II (from anodic oxidation of I)
β-hydroxy-CF2H styrene adduct (route B, H2O addition to cation)

electron_flow: Anode oxidizes CF2HSO2Na (1e-) to release SO2 and a CF2H radical, which adds to the electron-rich olefin to give a stabilized α-amino or benzylic radical I; I is further oxidized at the anode to carbocation II, which either undergoes E1 deprotonation (enamides, route A) or is trapped by water (styrenes, route B); 2H+ are reduced at the cathode to H2.

probe_reagents.name: cyclohexene (unactivated/non-stabilizing alkene control)
probe_reagents.observation: No reaction, indicating that a stabilized (α-amino or benzylic) radical I is required.

probe_reagents.name: alkyl-substituted (non-aryl, non-enamide) alkenes
probe_reagents.observation: Poorly reactive (<20% yield), supporting the need for radical stabilization.

probe_reagents.name: CF2HSO2Na (CV redox probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Shows an irreversible oxidation wave; enamide and styrene show no oxidation/reduction up to ~1.5 V, and mixtures with CF2HSO2Na give unchanged profiles, ruling out substrate oxidation and supporting sulfinate as the species oxidized at the anode.

notes: Two product-forming branches share a common cation II: route A (E1 elimination) for enamides gives a single (E)-vinyl-CF2H diastereomer; route B (nucleophilic addition of H2O) for styrenes gives β-hydroxy-CF2H products. No radical trapping (e.g., TEMPO/BHT) or DFT was reported; the mechanism is supported mainly by CV and control experiments with non-stabilizing alkenes.
